metadata.summary: A phase-and-budget decision map for selecting research tools across seven categories: exploration, competitor intel, user interviews, survey analysis, sentiment, synthesis, and synthetic research.

content.title: AI Research Tool Categories
content.summary: A phase-and-budget decision map for selecting research tools across seven categories: exploration, competitor intel, user interviews, survey analysis, sentiment, synthesis, and synthetic research. Agents use this map as a decision tree; given phase and budget, they output a stack recommendation with API availability flags.

why.p: The AI research tool market is dense and changes rapidly. Picking the wrong tool mid-sprint is expensive to reverse; tool sprawl beyond 3 tools per sprint creates coordination overhead that exceeds analytical benefit. A structured map with budget tiers and API-availability signals prevents both failure modes.

list.item: Planning a research stack at the start of a new project or research sprint.
Selecting tools for a specific research phase (discovery → synthesis → validation).
Budget-scoping a research operation (free vs. mid vs. enterprise).
Choosing the right tool per research question type before committing to a SaaS contract.
As a substitute for evaluating data privacy requirements — always check data processing agreements.
When the question is fully addressable with a single tool already in use — avoid tool sprawl.
For purely qualitative synthesis where Claude alone suffices — no additional tooling needed.
Exploration — discovery queries, competitive landscape, trend research: Perplexity, Claude, ChatGPT, Google Trends
Competitor Intel — feature tracking, messaging, funding: Crayon, Klue, Contify, AlphaSense
User Interviews — transcription, theme extraction, repositories: Looppanel, Dovetail, User Interviews, AssemblyAI
Survey Analysis — quantitative feedback, AI summaries: Qualtrics, SurveyMonkey AI
Sentiment — social listening, brand monitoring: Brandwatch, Sprout Social
Synthesis — affinity mapping, research repositories: Miro AI, Condens, EnjoyHQ, Dovetail
Synthetic Research — AI-generated profiles and responses: Synthetic Users, Viewpoints.ai, Aaru
Free/Low — Perplexity (free tier), Claude, Google Trends, Whisper (OSS), pytrends
Mid — Perplexity Pro, Looppanel, Dovetail, Condens, SurveyMonkey AI, AssemblyAI, Synthetic Users
Enterprise — AlphaSense, Klue, full Crayon, Qualtrics AI, EnjoyHQ, Brandwatch, Userlytics

rule.statement: Pick one canonical repository tool (Dovetail or Condens) and funnel all research outputs there; fragmented insights across tools is the primary cause of research debt.
rule.rationale: When insights live in Looppanel, Miro, Notion, and email threads simultaneously, patterns become invisible and research cannot be cited or reused.

rule.statement: Use no more than 3 tools per research sprint; coordination overhead exceeds analytical benefit beyond that threshold.
rule.rationale: Each additional tool adds context-switching, data format translation, and account management overhead that compounds over a sprint.

rule.statement: Prefer tools with REST APIs returning structured JSON for any phase where agents need to consume output; reject tools that produce only human-readable dashboards for agent pipelines.
rule.rationale: "Agent-friendly" means the API is designed for automation, not just that an API exists. Rate limits and output formats must be verified before pipeline design.

rule.statement: Audit your tool stack annually; the AI research tooling market consolidates rapidly and pricing changes affect stack viability.
rule.rationale: Tools acquired or sunset in the past year include several category leaders. Annual audits prevent paying for deprecated tools or missing better alternatives.

rule.statement: Use this category map as a decision tree: given phase and budget, select 1-2 tools per phase, then read phase-specific methodology files for execution details.
rule.rationale: The category map narrows tool selection; per-phase methodologies (e.g., ai-interview-analysis, synthetic-users) provide execution rules. Do not conflate navigation with execution.

rule.statement: Human confirmation required at tool selection stage; the agent recommends, the researcher confirms before any SaaS contract or pipeline build begins.
rule.rationale: Wrong tool choice is expensive to reverse mid-sprint. Tool-selection agents tend to recommend well-known tools, not the most API-accessible ones; researcher validates fit.

rule.statement: Verify API availability and rate limits independently before building a pipeline; "agent-friendly" in any reference does not guarantee automation suitability.
rule.rationale: Many SaaS tools have APIs that are poorly documented, rate-limited for automation, or require enterprise contracts to access programmatically.

rule.statement: For B2B user research with NDA constraints, verify data processing agreements before piping transcripts through any SaaS tool.
rule.rationale: Many SaaS research tools process data on third-party servers; participant confidentiality agreements may prohibit this without explicit consent.

rule.statement: Free/mid stacks cover 80% of solopreneur research needs; enterprise tools add competitive intelligence breadth, not user research depth.
rule.rationale: Enterprise tier tools (AlphaSense, Klue, Brandwatch) specialize in market and competitive intelligence. User research depth requires qualitative tools available at mid tier.

list.item: Perplexity MCP: Exploration phase: cited web synthesis (MCP server, perplexity.ai/api)
SimilarWeb CLI (unofficial): Competitor traffic estimates (similarweb.com/developer)
Google Trends (pytrends): Trend exploration, free tier (pip install pytrends / github.com/GeneralMills/pytrends)
Brandwatch CLI: Sentiment monitoring (enterprise) (brandwatch.com/developer)
Perplexity Pro (SaaS, Agent-friendly: Yes API): Exploration phase; best cited synthesis for free/mid
Claude / ChatGPT (SaaS, Agent-friendly: Yes API): Exploration + synthesis; calculation assistance; no live data
Crayon (SaaS, Agent-friendly: Yes API): Competitor intel; feature and messaging tracking
Klue (SaaS, Agent-friendly: Partial): Competitor intel; battlecards; enterprise contract required
Contify (SaaS, Agent-friendly: Partial): Competitor intel; news monitoring with API
AlphaSense (SaaS, Agent-friendly: Partial): Competitor intel + market sizing; financial filings
Looppanel (SaaS, Agent-friendly: Partial (API beta)): User interviews; transcription + insight tagging
Dovetail (SaaS, Agent-friendly: Yes API): User interviews + synthesis; repository with API
User Interviews (SaaS, Agent-friendly: No): Participant recruitment; no agent-accessible analysis layer
Qualtrics (SaaS, Agent-friendly: Yes API): Survey analysis; enterprise AI layer (Qualtrics AI)
SurveyMonkey AI (SaaS, Agent-friendly: Partial): Survey analysis; AI summaries; limited API for analysis output
Brandwatch (SaaS, Agent-friendly: Yes API): Sentiment monitoring; social listening
Sprout Social (SaaS, Agent-friendly: Yes API): Sentiment + social; stronger on publishing than analysis
Miro AI (SaaS, Agent-friendly: No): Synthesis / affinity mapping; no export API for AI outputs
Condens (SaaS, Agent-friendly: Yes API): Synthesis; research repository with structured tagging
EnjoyHQ (by UserZoom) (SaaS, Agent-friendly: Partial): Synthesis repository; enterprise
Synthetic Users (SaaS, Agent-friendly: Yes API): Synthetic research; profile + response simulation
Viewpoints.ai (SaaS, Agent-friendly: Partial): Synthetic research; concept validation
Google Trends (Free, Agent-friendly: Yes (unofficial pytrends)): Trend exploration; relative volume only

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: An agent uses this category map as a decision tree: given a research phase and budget, it selects 1-2 tools per phase and outputs a stack recommendation with integration notes. For agent-driven research pipelines, the agent prioritizes tools with REST APIs and structured output (JSON) over tools that produce only human-readable dashboards. The agent then reads phase-specific methodology files (e.g., ai-interview-analysis, synthetic-users) for execution details.

Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent (can be extended with tool-selection as a planning step before execution), General Claude subagent (Sonnet) for stack recommendation from phase + budget inputs, General Claude subagent (Haiku) for tool lookup and data retrieval from selected tools.

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.code:
I am running a [research type: discovery / validation / competitive] research sprint. Budget tier: [free / mid / enterprise]. Research phase: [exploration / interviews / synthesis / competitive intel]. Team size: [1 researcher / small team / large team].
Recommend a tool stack. For each tool: - Name and category - Why it fits this phase - API availability (yes/no) - One thing to watch out for Format as a markdown table.

Given this research question: "[question]" Which tool category is most appropriate and why? Options: Exploration | Competitor Intel | User Interviews | Survey Analysis | Sentiment | Synthesis | Synthetic Research. Return: category, top 2 tools, and the specific capability needed.

list.item: The tool landscape changes rapidly; this category map can be 6-12 months stale without active maintenance
Budget tiers vary by team size, geography, and negotiation; "mid" for one team is "enterprise" for another
Tool API availability does not equal agent suitability — many SaaS tools have APIs that are poorly documented or rate-limited for automation
No single tool covers all research phases well; the map forces choosing a stack, not a silver bullet
Pick one canonical repository tool (Dovetail or Condens) and funnel all research outputs there; fragmented insights across tools is the primary cause of research debt
Prefer tools with REST APIs that return structured JSON for any phase where agents need to consume output
Free/mid stacks cover 80% of solopreneur research needs; enterprise tools add breadth for competitive intelligence, not depth for user research
Audit your tool stack annually; the AI research tooling market consolidates rapidly and pricing changes affect stack viability
Never use more than 3 tools per research sprint; coordination overhead exceeds analytical benefit beyond that
"Agent-friendly" in the services table means a structured API exists; it does not mean the API is designed for automation — always check rate limits and output formats before building a pipeline
Many SaaS research tools process data on third-party servers; for B2B user research with NDA constraints, verify data processing agreements before piping transcripts through them
Tool-selection agents tend to recommend the most well-known tools, not the most API-accessible; always verify API availability independently before committing
Human checkpoint at tool selection stage: the agent recommends, the researcher confirms — wrong tool choice is expensive to reverse mid-sprint

template.purpose: Python: phase + budget → filtered tool list from the category map
Prompt for stack recommendation with API-availability and watch-out fields
